2026-05-12
[00:46:23.0148] <Justin Ridgewell>
This is a fun one not directly related to the spec, and all browsers agree on the behavior:
```
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Sectioned Source Maps Don't (always) Trigger Breakpoints</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Sectioned Source Maps Don't (always) Trigger Breakpoints</h1>
  <p>Open DevTools (F12) and check the Sources panel.</p>
  <p>Place a breakpoint on any line of the <code>calculate()</code> function of <code>source.js</code>. You'll notice that the breakpoint does not hit.</p>
  <p>Now toggle the output's script code so that <code>first()</code> is called. The breakpoint now hits.</p>
  <p>Even though both functions map to the same locations in <code>source.js</code>, placing a breakpoint on the source file will only place the breakpoint on the first sectioned source map that includes that source file.</p>
  <script>function first() {
  const foo = 1;
  const bar = 2;
  return foo + bar;
}
function second() {
  const foo = 1;
  const bar = 2;
  return foo + bar;
}
/*/
first();
/*/
second();
//*/
//# sourceMappingURL=data:application/json;charset=utf-8;base64,
//# sourceURL=output.js
</script>
</body>
</html>
```

[00:47:04.0870] <Justin Ridgewell>
Placing a breakpoint on the source file's line will not trigger breakpoints, because the source map is a sectioned map where both sections includes that source file.

[00:47:34.0147] <Justin Ridgewell>
We cannot just naively concatenate mappings together into a sectioned map.

[00:48:01.0221] <Justin Ridgewell>
Which has been very annoying with Google Search's internal source maps.

[00:50:05.0064] <Justin Ridgewell>
* This is a fun one not directly related to the spec, and all browsers agree on the behavior:

https://output.jsbin.com/tabojov/quiet

```
<!DOCTYPE html>
<html>
<head>
  <title>Sectioned Source Maps Don't (always) Trigger Breakpoints</title>
</head>
<body>
  <h1>Sectioned Source Maps Don't (always) Trigger Breakpoints</h1>
  <p>Open DevTools (F12) and check the Sources panel.</p>
  <p>Place a breakpoint on any line of the <code>calculate()</code> function of <code>source.js</code>. You'll notice that the breakpoint does not hit.</p>
  <p>Now toggle the output's script code so that <code>first()</code> is called. The breakpoint now hits.</p>
  <p>Even though both functions map to the same locations in <code>source.js</code>, placing a breakpoint on the source file will only place the breakpoint on the first sectioned source map that includes that source file.</p>
  <script>function first() {
  const foo = 1;
  const bar = 2;
  return foo + bar;
}
function second() {
  const foo = 1;
  const bar = 2;
  return foo + bar;
}
/*/
first();
/*/
second();
//*/
//# sourceMappingURL=data:application/json;charset=utf-8;base64,
//# sourceURL=output.js
</script>
</body>
</html>
```

[00:59:36.0163] <Justin Ridgewell>
https://evanw.github.io/source-map-visualization/#


2026-05-13
[03:55:07.0300] <Nic>
Hey, I didn't notice that the TG4 meeting is today. Let's cancel it, given that we are having it next week too?

[05:45:10.0571] <Caio Lima>
I have one question regarding this test case here: https://gist.github.com/caiolima/6043cdbfd4e57a39f079fd007675ff9d. There I have a situation where I have an expression `add(2, 3) + multiply(4, 5)` where we both inline and constant fold calls, resulting in a single constant `25` on generated code. The thing is that the expected GeneratedRange I crafted doesn't look correct, because I'm getting one sibling `start` (line: 0, col: 12)` beginning before its other sibling `end` (line: 0, col: 12). Using ChromeDev tools encoder, I'm getting the following error: `Attempting to encode range item that precedes the last encoded range item (0, 12)`.Have you faced a similar limitation? 

[05:45:27.0293] <Caio Lima>
* I have one question regarding this test case here: https://gist.github.com/caiolima/6043cdbfd4e57a39f079fd007675ff9d. There I have a situation where I have an expression `add(2, 3) + multiply(4, 5)` where we both inline and constant fold calls, resulting in a single constant `25` on generated code. The thing is that the expected GeneratedRange I crafted doesn't look correct, because I'm getting one sibling `start` (line: 0, col: 12)`beginning before its other sibling`end`(line: 0, col: 12). Using ChromeDev tools encoder, I'm getting the following error:`Attempting to encode range item that precedes the last encoded range item (0, 12)\`.

Have you faced a similar limitation? If so, what did you do to solve that?

[05:46:47.0527] <Caio Lima>
My intuition is that we should be able to represent that a given constant can be the result of multiple inlined calls constant folded.

[05:57:29.0921] <Caio Lima>
* I have one question regarding this test case here: https://gist.github.com/caiolima/6043cdbfd4e57a39f079fd007675ff9d. There I have a situation where I have an expression `add(2, 3) + multiply(4, 5)` where we both inline and constant fold calls, resulting in a single constant `25` on generated code. The thing is that the expected GeneratedRange I crafted doesn't look correct, because I'm getting one sibling `start` (line: 0, col: 12) beginning before its other sibling `end`(line: 0, col: 12). Using ChromeDev tools encoder, I'm getting the following error:`Attempting to encode range item that precedes the last encoded range item (0, 12)\`.

Have you faced a similar limitation? If so, what did you do to solve that?

[05:57:42.0068] <Caio Lima>
* I have one question regarding this test case here: https://gist.github.com/caiolima/6043cdbfd4e57a39f079fd007675ff9d. There I have a situation where I have an expression `add(2, 3) + multiply(4, 5)` where we both inline and constant fold calls, resulting in a single constant `25` on generated code. The thing is that the expected GeneratedRange I crafted doesn't look correct, because I'm getting one sibling `start` (line: 0, col: 12) beginning before its other sibling `end`(line: 0, col: 12). Using ChromeDev tools encoder, I'm getting the following error:`Attempting to encode range item that precedes the last encoded range item (0, 12)`.

Have you faced a similar limitation? If so, what did you do to solve that?

[05:59:39.0114] <Caio Lima>
The alternative I'm thinking so far is to actually either not emit the inline, or probably tweak the range to be a zero-range. However both of them I feel that we are missing interesting information here.

[06:00:09.0012] <Caio Lima>
* The alternative I'm thinking so far is to actually either don't emit the inline, or probably tweak the range for children to be a zero-range. However both of them I feel that we are missing interesting information here.

[06:02:40.0743] <Caio Lima>
I'm also curious to know what debuggers would do if they face such condition, because having the same range for 2 different "GeneratedRange" and different callsites is something I'm having a hard time to think how a debugger could show that.

[06:27:30.0754] <Holger Benl>
That's an interesting example. I'd omit the inlining information here because I wouldn't even know what the debugger should do with that information: if you paused at `25` in the generated code, what would you expect the debugger to show?

[06:28:38.0016] <Holger Benl>
Generally I'd always start with the question: what should the debugger show if I'm paused here and then think about what the scope map has to look like to achieve that.

[06:29:06.0439] <Holger Benl>
We don't pretend we can support every possible scenario with scope maps and that's OK.


2026-05-15
[03:26:43.0607] <Simon Zünd>
Caio Lima: re your example. I didn't think it through in-depth but I agree with holger. The interesting bit is that a debugger can not even pause on the `25`. At most it would pause on the `console.log` call. The `25` itself can also not cause a JS Error (with a stack trace) unless it's a syntax error and those don't have a stack. So whatever info terser emits there would be ignored by most debuggers anyway, at which point terser can skip emitting range info for trivial things like literals.

[13:08:31.0667] <Nic>
If a transform preserves an identifier as-is, is there ever a reason to put the name in the mapping?

[13:10:58.0058] <Nic>
Also, for https://github.com/babel/babel/issues/14907, if the function is not renamed, is there any reason to put the name in the mapping for `(`?

[14:01:23.0565] <Simon Zünd>
Chrome DevTools falls back to whatever the AST put as the name if no named mapping exist: https://source.chromium.org/chromium/chromium/src/+/main:third_party/devtools-frontend/src/front_end/core/sdk/SourceMapScopesInfo.ts;l=93;drc=5717d940b6563e8f5926cd8428423e7d588e3200

2026-05-25
[00:52:08.0387] <Simon Zünd>
Heads up: Stephen Adams from the Dart team will join the "scopes" meeting this Thursday (28th). I had a chat with him before the Amsterdam meeting and they expressed some interest in the "scopes" proposal for the Dart to Js / Dart to Wasm compilers. He has filed some issues on our GitHub since then so i thought it would be great to hear about their use-cases/problems in-person.

[01:08:02.0177] <Simon Zünd>
* Heads up: Stephen Adams from the Dart team will join the "scopes" meeting this Thursday (28th). I had a chat with him before the Amsterdam meeting and they expressed some interest in the "scopes" proposal for the Dart to Js / Dart to Wasm compilers. He has filed some issues on our GitHub since then so i thought it would be great to chat about their use-cases/problems in-person.

[03:01:51.0392] <Aki>
I would love to chat with Stephen some time if you could do an introduction? Ecma is wondering when Dart is planning to publish its next version https://ecma-international.org/publications-and-standards/standards/ecma-408/
